features.summary: NVIDIA reported record Q4 2021 revenue of $5.0 billion, up 61% year-over-year, with full-year revenue reaching $16.7 billion, up 53%. Gaming revenue hit a record $2.5 billion in Q4, driven by strong demand for GeForce RTX 30 Series GPUs based on Ampere architecture. Data center revenue was $1.9 billion, up 97% year-over-year including Mellanox, with A100 adoption accelerating across hyperscalers and vertical industries. The company introduced cryptocurrency mining processors (CMPs) to address mining demand while preserving GeForce GPUs for gamers, estimating crypto contributed $100-300 million to Q4 gaming revenue.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q1 2022 revenue of $5.3 billion with most sequential growth from gaming, while all platforms are expected to grow quarter-over-quarter. The company sees multi-year growth ahead driven by the early stages of Ampere architecture adoption across gaming and data center. Management highlighted entering the third phase of AI industrialization, with autonomous systems and edge computing creating new multi-billion dollar opportunities. The Arm acquisition process is progressing as expected with regulatory reviews ongoing, positioning NVIDIA for the age of AI computing.
features.risks: Supply constraints remain a company-wide challenge with demand exceeding supply, though management expects sufficient supply to meet guidance and grow throughout the year. Cryptocurrency mining demand creates uncertainty for gaming GPU availability, though the new CMP product line aims to address this. The Arm acquisition faces regulatory scrutiny across multiple jurisdictions with an 18-month approval timeline. Gaming channel inventories remain low and are expected to stay constrained through Q1, potentially limiting near-term growth.
features.key_drivers: Gaming growth is driven by the RTX 30 Series upgrade cycle with only 15% of the GeForce installed base upgraded to RTX, plus expanding gamer and creator markets reaching 45 million creators. Data center growth is fueled by A100 universal GPU adoption for AI training, inference, and HPC across hyperscalers and vertical industries including financial services, healthcare, and scientific computing. New growth vectors include BlueField DPUs for data center security, 5G edge AI applications, and autonomous systems across industries from automotive to retail, supported by expanding software platforms like Omniverse.
